coredata.dc:title: Silver nanoprism-mediated colourimetric sensing probe for efficient detection of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in water and reuse of formed bimetallic nanoprisms

Kim, Mun Ho
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and selective methods for detecting Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in water are crucial for environmental monitoring and remediation. Although traditional methods for detection of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions are accurate and sensitive, they face substantial challenges due to high costs, reliance on specialised equipment and limited field applicability, thereby presenting notable limitations. In this study, we introduce a novel colourimetric sensing probe designed specifically to identify Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in aqueous solutions. This probe utilises the enhanced chemical stability of Ag nanoprisms achieved through Pd or Pt deposition on their surfaces. Our approach features exceptionally low limits of detection of 2.6 nM for Pd(II) and 0.3 nM for Pt(II), indicating an impressive detection range. Furthermore, the probe’s ease of use, cost-effectiveness and compatibility with both naked eye and UV–Vis spectrophotometric detection make it a selective, reliable and affordable option for point-of-care analysis. Beyond its impressive sensitivity for ion detection, this methodology offers the additional benefit of enabling the on-demand synthesis of customised bimetallic catalysts. The synthesised Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt bimetallic nanoprisms demonstrate promising catalytic potential for environmental remediation. This advancement paves the way for efficient recycling and reuse of valuable Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in various catalytic applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) A visual depiction and (b) UV–Vis–NIR extinction spectra of aqueous colloidal dispersions of Ag nanoprisms following a 20 min exposure to 0.3 mM NaBr at room temperature. (c-f) TEM images of Ag nanoprisms treated to 0.3 mM NaBr for different durations: (c) 0 min, (d) 3 min, (e) 15 min and (f) 20 min, respectively.

(a) UV–Vis–NIR extinction spectra of colloidal dispersions containing Ag nanoprism reacted with Pd2+ observed for 90 min at room temperature. (b)-(c) STEM–EDS elemental mapping images of Ag nanoprisms colloidal dispersions reacted with Pd2+ at room temperature for (b) 30 min and (c) 90 min, respectively.

(a) A visual depiction and (b) UV–Vis–NIR extinction spectra of aqueous Ag/Pd hybrid nanoprism colloidal dispersions at various time intervals, extending up to 20 min, following exposure to 0.3 mM NaBr at room temperature. (c-d) TEM images of Ag/Pd hybrid nanoprisms subjected to 0.3 mM NaBr, captured at (c) 9 min and (d) 20 min.

(a) UV–Vis–NIR extinction spectra illustrating alterations in the LSPR properties of Ag nanoprisms within colloidal dispersions that have been incubated with Pd(II) ions at various concentrations for 30 min and exposed to 0.3 mM NaBr for 20 min. (b) A graphical representation illustrates the wavelength shift (Δλ) relative to Pd(II) ion concentration. The data for this plot are derived from the extinction spectra shown in panel (a). Inset in panel (b) is a photographic depiction of Ag nanoprisms in colloidal dispersions incubated with various concentrations of Pd(II) ions, ranging from 0.025 to 10 μM and subsequently exposed to 0.3 mM NaBr.

(a) (a) UV–Vis–NIR extinction spectra of colloidal dispersions containing Ag nanoprism recorded after exposure to 3.9 µM Pd(II) ions and a variety of other ions, including heavy metals and halides, for 30 min, followed by exposure to 0.3 mM NaBr for 20 min at room temperature. (b) A plot of the Δλ values from (a). The inset in (b) visually depicts aqueous Ag nanoprisms colloidal dispersions subjected to NaBr exposure in the presence of 3.9 µM Pd(II) ions and other ions.
(a) TEM and (b) STEM–EDS elemental mapping images of Ag nanoprisms colloidal dispersions incubated with 20 µM Pt(II) ions in room temperature for 30 min.

(a) Photomicrograph and (b) UV–Vis–NIR spectra of aqueous dispersions containing Ag/Pt hybrid nanoprism, subjected to 0.3 mM sodium bromide (NaBr) at room temperature for different durations, extending up to 30 min. In addition, (c-d) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of Ag/Pt hybrid nanoprisms following exposure to 0.3 mM NaBr, with (c) capturing the morphology after a 10 min exposure and (d) illustrating the alterations observed after 25 min of exposure.

(a) UV–Vis–NIR extinction spectra of aqueous dispersions containing Ag nanoprism in response to varying concentrations of Pt(II) ions (nM) and exposure to 0.3 mM sodium bromide (NaBr) for 20 min. (b) A plot of wavelength shift (Δλ) against Pt(II) ions concentration, derived from the data in panel (a). The inset of (b) presents a photographic representation of Ag nanoprisms colloidal dispersions incubated with different concentrations of Pt(II) ions (nM) and exposed to 0.3 mM NaBr.

(a) UV/Vis/NIR extinction spectra of aqueous dispersions containing Ag nanoprisms with various concentrations of Pt ions (0.55, 1.1, 2.2 and 4.4 µM) in drinking water exposed to 0.75 mM NaBr at 60 °C for 8 min. (b) Wavelength shifts (correspond to (a)) of the various Ag nanoprisms colloidal dispersion containing different concentrations of Pt(II) in drinking water and DI water system.

head.ce:title: Silver nanoprism-mediated colourimetric sensing probe for efficient detection of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in water and reuse of formed bimetallic nanoprisms

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Engineering, Pukyong National University, 45 Yongso-ro, Nam-gu, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer Engineering, Pukyong National University, 45 Yongso-ro, Nam-gu, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and selective methods for detecting Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in water are crucial for environmental monitoring and remediation. Although traditional methods for detection of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions are accurate and sensitive, they face substantial challenges due to high costs, reliance on specialised equipment and limited field applicability, thereby presenting notable limitations. In this study, we introduce a novel colourimetric sensing probe designed specifically to identify Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in aqueous solutions. This probe utilises the enhanced chemical stability of Ag nanoprisms achieved through Pd or Pt deposition on their surfaces. Our approach features exceptionally low limits of detection of 2.6 nM for Pd(II) and 0.3 nM for Pt(II), indicating an impressive detection range. Furthermore, the probe’s ease of use, cost-effectiveness and compatibility with both naked eye and UV–Vis spectrophotometric detection make it a selective, reliable and affordable option for point-of-care analysis. Beyond its impressive sensitivity for ion detection, this methodology offers the additional benefit of enabling the on-demand synthesis of customised bimetallic catalysts. The synthesised Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt bimetallic nanoprisms demonstrate promising catalytic potential for environmental remediation. This advancement paves the way for efficient recycling and reuse of valuable Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in various catalytic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The accurate detection of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in water is crucial in various industrial and environmental contexts [1]. Pd and Pt are valuable metals used in catalysis, electronics and jewellery, among other fields. Their increasing usage raises concerns about environmental pollution and health risks [2]. Chronic exposure to low levels of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions, recognised as carcinogens, can lead to adverse health impacts [3,4]. Furthermore, the presence of these ions in drinking water sources heightens health worries, emphasising the need for a reliable sensing mechanism to detect and measure these ions effectively [5]. This platform is crucial for effective environmental monitoring and the protection of human health. Trace amounts of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in water contaminated with these elements are typically identified using established laboratory techniques such as inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP–AES), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP–MS) and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [6–8]. Although these traditional methods are effective, they often necessitate skilled personnel, complex analytical procedures and extensive sample preparation [9]. Consequently, there has been a push towards developing alternative techniques, including electrochemical and fluorescence detection [9–11]. Nevertheless, these alternatives are hindered by their complexities, relatively high costs and limited applicability for on-site testing. Hence, finding a user-friendly, cost-effective and field-applicable technique for detecting Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in water remains an urgent and unresolved issue.

The employment of anisotropic Ag nanostructures, especially triangular Ag nanoprisms, has attracted significant attention due to their exceptional sensitivity to local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [12–14]. This heightened sensitivity is attributed to their ability to concentrate the electromagnetic field near their pointed tips, making them valuable for various applications [15]. However, despite their promising attributes, these materials have critical limitations that impede their widespread use, mainly due to inherent structural weaknesses [16]. A major concern is the limited stability of Ag nanoprisms, linked to their structural properties. Specifically, the sharp tips of these nanoprisms are particularly prone to etching in the presence of halide ions, polyelectrolytes or oxidising agents in aqueous environments [17–20]. This morphological change compromises the nanoprisms’ stability and degrades their original optical properties [21]. The sharpness of nanoprisms’ tips is closely associated with the resonance mode’s peak position, negatively affecting their optical functionality. Therefore, addressing the challenge of enhancing the chemical stability of Ag nanoprisms with sharp tips is crucial for expanding their practical applications.

Bimetallic nanocrystals have garnered considerable interest in nanoscience owing to their distinctive characteristics and superior stability compared to monometallic nanocrystals [22,23]. Their increased stability is attributed to synergistic effects from the atomic-level combination of two distinct metal components [24]. The alloying process entails blending these metals at the atomic scale, resulting in a structured and orderly atomic configuration. This configuration tends to be more resistant to corrosion or oxidation as the dual metal presence modifies the electronic structure and surface traits, thereby augmenting the nanocrystals’ resistance to chemical degradation [25]. This improved stability is particularly beneficial in applications where nanocrystals face extreme chemical environments and high-temperature conditions.

In this study, we developed a user-friendly sensing probe to rapidly detect Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in aqueous media. This innovative approach is centred on a newly developed spontaneous chemical etching technique tailored specifically for Ag nanoprisms known for their exceptional plasmonic properties.

The interaction between Ag nanoprisms and Pd(II) or Pt(II) ions in a solution is pivotal. Due to the relatively low reduction potential of Ag nanoprisms, they are likely to be replaced by Pd(II) or Pt(II) ions, which have higher reduction potentials [26]. This interaction leads to the spontaneous deposition of Pd or Pt on the surface of Ag nanoprisms. Consequently, the resulting Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt bimetallic nanoprisms exhibit significantly enhanced chemical stability, maintaining their original shape even when exposed to strong etchants. The kinetics of colour and spectral changes in aqueous dispersions of bimetallic nanoprisms, when exposed to a potent etchant, were found to strongly depend on the concentrations of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in the water. Leveraging this correlation, we developed an innovative sensing probe for the selective and sensitive detection of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in water, yielding results visible to the naked eye.

The colourimetric probe proposed here offers distinct advantages in detecting Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions. It utilises the vivid colour transformations of Ag nanoprisms due to size and shape alterations and their remarkable, tuneable LSPR properties. These conspicuous colour changes facilitate on-site detection without specialised equipment. Moreover, the probe is portable, user-friendly and has an extended shelf life, simplifying the detection process as it requires neither sophisticated equipment nor extensive sample preparation. Beyond detecting ions, this methodology allows the on-demand synthesis of tailored bimetallic catalysts. The synthesised Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt bimetallic nanoprisms show promising catalytic potential, opening avenues for efficient recycling and reuse of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in various catalytic applications [23,27,28]. Indeed, the catalytic efficacy of the synthesised bimetallic nanoprisms was assessed by the reduction of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP), using NaBH4 as a reducing agent in a model reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Chemicals and materials
ce:section.ce:para: Potassium tetrachloropalladate (II) (K2PdCl4), potassium tetrachloroplatinate (II) (K2PtCl4), acetonitrile, polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw ≈ 29 kDa) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Other chemicals including polyacrylamide (PAM, Mw ≈ 10 kDa), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2⋅6H2O), aluminium (III) nitrate nonahydrate (Al(NO3)3⋅9H2O), cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2⋅4H2O), gallium (III) nitrate hydrate (Ga(NO3)3⋅xH2O), copper acetate (Cu(CH3CO2)2), iron (III) nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3⋅9H2O), lithium nitrate (LiNO3), sodium bromide (NaBr, 310506, 100 g), sodium chloride (NaCl), magnesium acetate tetrahydrate (Mg(CH3CO2)2⋅4H2O), sodium fluoride (NaF), nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2), zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(CH3CO2)2⋅2H2O), 4-NP and NaBH4, were also sourced from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Barium nitrate (Ba(NO3)2) was procured from Junsei Chemical (Japan). Deionised water (DI) with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ was produced using a Millipore deionised water system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ag nanoprisms synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Ag nanoprisms were prepared following established protocols [14]. The synthesis was conducted in an aqueous medium, with PVP acting as both a mild reducing agent and a colloidal stabilizer. Notably, the inclusion of PAM and acetonitrile played a critical role in forming stable coordination complexes with Ag+ ions. This coordination complexation significantly retarded the Ag+ reduction process, thereby lowering the steady-state concentration of metallic Ag(0). Consequently, this approach facilitated the high-yield and size-uniform generation of Ag nanoprisms. In a more detailed procedure, aqueous solutions containing 5 wt% PAM (0.1 g), polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) (1.870 g) and deionised (DI) water (8 mL) were prepared in a 20 mL vial. These were uniformly stirred using a magnetic stirrer. Subsequently, pure acetonitrile (1 mL) was added to the vial, followed by the injection of a 282 mM AgNO3 aqueous solution (2 mL). The mixture was then magnetically stirred at 80 °C for 24 h. After cooling, the mixture underwent centrifugation at 13,000 rpm for 10 min. The resulting product was washed meticulously three times with deionised water to ensure the removal of any residual solvents and PVP.

This efficient one-pot strategy for fabricating Ag nanoprisms eliminates the need for pre-synthesized seed particles and enables scalable production without specialized equipment. Unlike traditional methods, often employing potentially hazardous reducing agents (e.g., NaBH4, hydrazine) and organic solvents (e.g., DMF), this approach utilizes the non-toxic reducing agent (PVP) in an aqueous medium, aligning with our commitment to sustainability and promoting safer and more accessible nanoparticle synthesis. Precise control over reaction conditions facilitates the synthesis of highly uniform and stable Ag nanoprisms, which exhibit exceptional optical properties, including tunable LSPR properties, rendering them promising candidates for cutting-edge colourimetric sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Pd(II) ions and Pt(II) ions
ce:section.ce:para: An aqueous solution of Ag nanoprisms (0.4 mg Ag NPs, 2 mL) was added to a 5 mL vial. Subsequently, a one-shot addition of an aqueous solution of Pd(II) ions K2PdCl4 (200 uL) at a specified concentration was made to the Ag nanoprisms solution. The resulting mixture was then incubated for 30 min at room temperature. After the 30 min incubation, 0.3 mM NaBr (200 uL) was introduced to the mixture. The resulting solution was then carefully adjusted to a final volume of 2.4 mL. To detect Pt(II) ions, a comparable methodology to that employed for Pd(II) ions was implemented, substituting K2PtCl4 at a specified concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity evaluation of the Ag nanoprisms-based sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the Ag nanoprism-based sensing mechanism toward Pd2+ ions was evaluated by exposing the probes to a variety of potentially interfering metal ions and halide anions. These included heavy metals (Pd2+, Cd2+, Ga3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Li2+, Zn2+) and halides (F−, Cl−). To assess the probe's specificity, each ion was spiked into the Ag nanoprism solution at a final concentration of 3.9 μM, matching the conditions used for Pd2+ and Pt2+ testing. Briefly, 200 μL aliquots of each ion solution, prepared in deionized water, were added to vials containing 2 mL of the Ag nanoprism colloidal dispersion (0.4 mg Ag NPs). Following a 30 min incubation at room temperature, 200 μL of a 0.3 mM NaBr solution was introduced to each vial. The mixtures were then incubated for an additional 20 min, and their colorimetric and UV–vis–NIR spectral responses were monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of Pt(II) ions in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of Pt(II) ions in real water samples was tested using drinking water spiked with K2PtCl4 at specified concentrations. For the detection, a 2 mL colloidal dispersion of Ag nanoprisms (0.4 mg Ag NPs) served as a probe for sensing platinum ions in the water. This procedure involved the addition of 200 µL of drinking water containing a predetermined amount of platinum ions to the Ag nanoprisms solution. Following this addition, the mixture was incubated for 5 min at room temperature. Subsequently, 0.75 mM NaBr (200 µL) was added to the mixture, which was then heated in an oven for 8 min at 60 °C. The method involving the addition of 0.75 mM NaBr and heating the mixture at 60 °C for 8 min was specifically designed based on our findings regarding the interaction between Ag nanoprisms and Pt(II) ions to optimize the sensing probe's performance for real-world applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic application for hydrogenation of 4-NP by NaBH4
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of hybrid metal Ag/Pd nanoprisms involved a precise addition of a 240 μM K2PdCl4 solution (200 μL) to a colloidal suspension of Ag nanoprisms (0.4 mg Ag NPs, 2.2 mL) in a vial. This process facilitated the deposition of Pd onto the surface of the Ag nanoprisms, thereby forming Ag/Pd bimetallic structures. The mixture was incubated at room temperature for 30 min to ensure adequate reaction time for the formation of the hybrid nanoparticles. Following incubation, the mixture underwent centrifugation at 13,000 rpm for 10 min to isolate the nanoparticles. The collected nanoparticle precipitate was then redispersed in deionized water to prepare it for subsequent catalytic applications, with the solution's final volume adjusted to 2.4 mL. This procedure resulted in a final molarity of Pd(II) ions being set at 20 μM. A similar synthesis procedure was applied to create hybrid metal Ag/Pt nanoprisms, with K2PtCl4 replacing K2PdCl4 as Pt source. This approach ensured the formation of Ag/Pt bimetallic nanoprisms through a comparable mechanism.

The catalytic activity of the synthesized bimetallic nanoprisms was evaluated through the reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP. This reaction was carried out in a 4.5 mL quartz cuvette at room temperature. Initially, 50 μL of a 1 mM 4-NP aqueous solution was introduced to the cuvette containing 1.3 mL of deionized (DI) water, followed by the addition of 200 μL of a 100 mM NaBH4 solution. This mixture created an excess of the reducing agent to ensure the reaction's completion. Then, 0.02 mg (100 μL) of the colloidal dispersion of either Ag, Ag/Pd, or Ag/Pt nanoprisms was added as a catalyst. The progress of the reaction was monitored by recording the UV–Vis absorption spectrum at defined intervals, providing insight into the catalytic efficiency of the nanoprisms by observing changes in the absorbance peaks corresponding to 4-NP and 4-AP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisations
ce:section.ce:para: The FEI Tecnai F30 transmission electron microscope, operating at 300 kV, was utilised to capture detailed transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images.

Subsequent characterisation included field-emission (FE)–TEM (EM-2100F, Jeol) with elemental mapping analysis. TEM sample preparation entailed carefully depositing the final aqueous solutions onto specialised carbon-coated copper grids. These samples were initially dried in a convection oven, followed by a DI water rinse to eliminate residual solvents and subsequently dried again. Room-temperature UV–Vis–NIR spectral analysis was meticulously conducted using the Cary 50 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Chemical stability of Ag nanoprisms and Ag/Pd bimetallic nanoprisms
Chemical stability of Ag nanoprisms
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized nanostructures, as shown in Fig. S1(a), displayed predominantly triangular shapes, and the average edge length was approximately 70 nm. In an aqueous solution, these nanoprisms showed a subtle grey colour, as seen in the inset of Fig. S1(b). The UV–Vis–NIR spectrum, presented in Fig. S1(b), revealed a peak of maximum extinction at 809 nm, aligning with the in-plane dipole LSPR mode typical for Ag nanoprisms [29,30]. Ag nanoprisms prepared using the synthetic method described in this study are significantly susceptible to chemical etching when exposed to halide ions, rendering them highly reactive in such settings. Notably, bromide ions (Br−) are especially effective as chemical etchants, leading to the swift etching of Ag nanoprisms [16]. This effect is frequently harnessed in nanoscience and nanotechnology for controlled alterations in the shape and size of Ag nanoprisms, profoundly impacting their optical characteristics. When a 0.3 mM NaBr solution was applied to an aqueous dispersion of Ag nanoprisms, it resulted in a NaBr/Ag molar ratio of approximately 1/5. This addition precipitated a swift and pronounced colour transition from grey to orange within a brief 20 min period, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 (a). Simultaneously, Fig. 1(b) illustrates the UV–Vis–NIR spectra monitoring, which shows a notable blue shift in wavelength, moving from 814 nm to 499 nm. The evident wavelength shift indicates changes in the shape and dimensions of the Ag nanoprisms following NaBr introduction [15,31]. Indeed, the TEM images in Fig. 1(c)-(f) provide clear visual confirmation of the gradual shift of Ag nanoprisms into spherical formations. This change emphasises the vulnerability of the apex regions of Ag nanoprisms to etching, resulting in substantial morphological alterations. Notably, the apex regions of Ag nanoprisms are characterised by distinct features, such as increased surface energy and a specific atomic arrangement, differing from the bulk areas of the nanoparticle [21,32]. These factors enhance their reactivity. In addition, coverage of PVP at the tip regions further elevates the reactivity of the atoms in these areas, rendering them more prone to chemical etching reactions [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical stability of Ag/Pd bimetallic nanoprisms
ce:section.ce:para:
A straightforward and rapid method for fabricating bimetallic nanocrystals utilises the galvanic replacement reaction [26,34,35]. Pd(II) ions exhibit a higher reduction potential than Ag+, a disparity that facilitates the spontaneous exchange of Ag atoms with Pd atoms during the reaction. Pd(II) ions are reduced to Pd atoms on the surface of Ag nanocrystals, simultaneously oxidising Ag atoms to Ag+. This process effectively transforms solid Ag nanocrystals into Ag/Pd bimetallic nanostructures, as demonstrated in the following reaction [36]:
(1) 2Ag + PdCl4 2− → 2Ag+ + Pd + 4Cl−

When Ag nanoprisms undergo galvanic replacement with Pd2+, the Pd layer on the surface may serve as a protective barrier, shielding the underlying Ag from etching by bromide. Consequently, this mechanism potentially improves the stability and durability of Ag/Pd hybrid nanoprisms when exposed to bromide.

The stability impact of Pd alloying on Ag nanoprisms was examined by introducing Pd(II) ions into an aqueous dispersion of these nanoprisms (Pd2+/Ag molar ratio ≈ 1/77). Following the Pd(II) ion addition, the dispersion was stirred and allowed to settle at room temperature for 90 min. During the initial 30 min, a notable redshift in the LSPR properties of the Ag nanoprisms occurred, shifting to 835 nm, indicative of Pd deposition on the Ag nanoprisms via a galvanic replacement reaction, as shown in Fig. 2 (a). No significant LSPR changes were observed after the first 30 min. STEM-energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (STEM–EDS) elemental mapping analysis confirmed the uniform Pd distribution on the Ag nanoprisms, as illustrated in Fig. 2(b). These hybrid nanoprisms displayed a Pd to Ag atomic ratio of approximately 7.8:92.2. Analysis of the sample after a 90 min reaction, shown in Fig. 2(c), revealed slight differences from Fig. 2(b), with a slight increase in Pd ratio to 8.7. Consequently, Ag/Pd hybrid nanoprisms formed during the 30 min interaction with Pd(II) were selected for further study.

Fig. 3 (a) shows that Ag/Pd hybrid nanoprism dispersions exhibited no colour change upon exposure to 0.3 mM NaBr for up to 20 min. This observation deviates from the pronounced colour changes observed in aqueous dispersions of Ag nanoprisms, as illustrated in Fig. 1(a). The UV/Vis/NIR extinction spectra of Ag/Pd hybrid nanoprism dispersions showed only slight alterations, with minimal impact on the LSPR properties, even after 60 min of exposure to NaBr, as shown in Fig. 3(b). TEM images of Ag/Pd hybrid nanoprisms exposed to NaBr for 9 and 20 min, respectively, confirmed that during the exposure, the nanoprisms consistently maintained their triangular morphology with well-defined corners (Fig. 3(c) and 3(d)). These findings conclusively show that hybridising Ag nanoprisms with Pd effectively shields them from chemical etching by NaBr.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colourimetric sensing of Pd(II) ions in water
Development of colourimetric sensing probe to detect Pd(II)
ce:section.ce:para: Given the enhanced chemical stability of Ag/Pd hybrid nanoprisms, the extent of etching in Ag nanoprisms may vary depending on the Pd(II) concentration in aqueous media, while the Br− concentration and reaction time remain constant. As Pd(II) concentration increases, more Pd atoms integrate into the nanoprisms, augmenting the surface coverage of Pd. This increased surface coverage creates a protective layer, safeguarding the underlying Ag component from chemical reactions and thus boosting the overall stability of the nanoprisms. In addition, the colour change of Ag nanoprisms in response to Br− shows variations, depending on the Pd(II) ion concentration in the solution.

The impact of Pd(II) ions on the etching rate of Ag nanoprisms in Br− solutions was explored. This involved incubating Ag nanoprisms with different concentrations of Pd(II) ions, which ranged from 0.025 to 10 μM, for 30 min at room temperature. After this, the samples were treated with 0.3 mM NaBr for 20 min at the same temperature. UV–Vis–NIR spectra were then recorded. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the in-plane dipole resonance mode showed a decreasing blueshift trend correlating with higher Pd(II) ion concentrations. Fig. 4(b) inset photograph illustrates aqueous dispersions of Ag nanoprisms treated with 0.3 mM Br− after interacting with varying Pd(II) ion levels. The dispersion colours shifted across a range, including orange, purple, blue, cyan and grey, in accordance with the increasing concentration of Pd(II) ions.

To quantitatively assess the impact of Pd(II) ion concentration, the shift value (Δλ) was calculated. This value indicates the difference in the in-plane dipole resonance mode between Pd(II)-free and Pd(II)-enriched dispersions. This analysis revealed a rise in the Δλ value in line with an increase in Pd(II) ion concentration, as shown in Fig. 4(b). Error bars on the curve represent standard deviation based on three iterations of the same experiment, ensuring the reliability of the results. These findings indicate that Ag nanoprisms exhibited distinct colour changes corresponding to Pd(II) ion concentrations in the range of 0.025–10 μM. The colour shift was visually noticeable and quantifiable through the measurement of wavelength shifts (Δλ). The findings provide promising evidence for developing a colourimetric sensing probe to detect Pd(II), achieved through the Br−-induced chemical etching of Ag nanoprisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of colourimetric sensing probe to detect Pd(II)
ce:section.ce:para: The limit of detection (LOD) is a key parameter for confirming the effectiveness of a sensing probe [31,37,38,5]. Fig. S2 illustrates a strong linear relationship between the change in wavelength (Δλ) and the Pd(II) concentration, represented by the equation y = 893.8x, where ‘y’ and ‘x’ to the Pd(II) concentration. This correlation highlights a significant association and provides insights into the concentration-dependent behaviour of Pd(II) in our experiments. A robust regression coefficient of 0.947 was obtained for the Pd(II) concentration range from 0 to 0.1 μM. The LOD for Pd(II) ions, determined using the developed sensing probe, was approximately 2.6 nM. It was calculated using a signal-to-noise ratio, defined as 3.3 times the standard deviation of the blank sample divided by the linear plot’s slope, resulting in 3.3 × (0.7 [nm]/893.8 [μM/nm]) [39–41]. Table S1 presents a detailed comparative analysis between this new detection system and existing methods for quantifying Pd(II) ions in aqueous environments. The analysis aims to place the performance and characteristics of the current system in the context of existing Pd(II) detection methods in the literature. Notably, this method offers an expanded detection range (0.0–10 μM) and an exceptional LOD, as detailed in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of colourimetric sensing probe to detect Pd(II)
ce:section.ce:para: Sensing selectivity is a crucial parameter in a comprehensive sensing system. Achieving high selectivity is essential for the sensing platform, particularly in detecting Pd(II) ions. It is vital that the Ag nanoprisms solution colour closely matches the blank sample when exposed to other ions. For selectivity assessment, various ions were tested, including cations (Cd2+, Ga3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Li2+, Zn2+) and anions (F−, Cl−). These ions were introduced at a concentration of 3.9 µM, mirroring the safety limit for Pd(II) ions in water, with a molar ratio of each ion to Ag of approximately 1/400. The UV–Vis–NIR spectra illustrating these findings are shown in Fig. 5 (a). The LSPR properties of Ag nanoprisms, when exposed to Pd(II) ions, exhibited a subtle blue shift, with wavelengths measured at 736 nm. In contrast, the wavelengths associated with other ions demonstrated a more pronounced blue shift, notably reaching around 500 nm after exposure to 0.3 mM NaBr for 20 min at room temperature. The wavelength shift (Δλ) for each sample was determined by comparison with the blank sample, which displayed a shift of 496 nm, as depicted in Fig. 5(b). A significant wavelength shift (Δλ) was noted in the presence of Pd(II) ions. Furthermore, as depicted in inset (b), the Ag nanoprisms colloidal dispersion exhibited a cyan colour exclusively in the presence of Pd(II) ions, whereas other ions induced colours varying from pink to orange. Without Pd(II) ions, the Ag nanoprisms’ colloidal dispersion showed a colouration akin to that of the blank sample. This distinction enables easy differentiation and highlights the remarkable selectivity for Pd(II) ions over other ions, discernible even to the naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colourimetric sensing of Pt(II) ions in water
Chemical stability of Ag/Pt bimetallic nanoprisms
ce:section.ce:para: Pt, a noble platinum-group element, is renowned for its exceptional catalytic efficiency and chemical properties similar to Pd [42]. Pt also has a significantly higher reduction potential compared to Ag, as indicated by its standard electrode potential (E°(Pt2+/Pt) = 1.18 V) being greater than that of Ag (E°(Ag+/Ag) = 0.799 V) [43]. Building on our research on Ag/Pd hybrid nanoprisms, we thoroughly investigated bimetallic nanoprisms containing both Ag and Pt. This study evaluated their potential use in a specialised system for sensitively detecting Pt(II) ions in aqueous environments.

The TEM image in Fig. 6 (a) highlights noticeable morphological alterations in Ag nanoprisms’ colloidal dispersions after incubating for 30 min with 20 µM Pt(II) ions at room temperature. This change is due to galvanic replacement (Pt2+/Ag molar ratio ≈ 1/77). Despite these changes, the Ag/Pt nanoprisms retain their distinctive triangular shape, with small openings appearing along their edges. A low-magnification TEM image of these nanoprisms is presented in Fig. S3(a). To investigate the reasons for these morphological transformations, we performed STEM–EDS elemental mapping, shown in Fig. 6(b), on the Ag/Pt nanoprisms post a 30 min reaction. This mapping indicated a consistent layering of Pt on the surface of the Ag nanoprisms, albeit with some variation in evenness. We determined the atomic composition ratio of Ag/Pt in the nanoprisms to be 2.2/97.8. In the galvanic replacement reaction under similar conditions, the lesser amount of Pt hybridisation compared to Pd is likely due to the greater lattice mismatch between Ag and Pt than between Ag and Pd, as referenced in [44]. Owing to this low Pt ratio, the LSPR properties of the Ag–Pt nanoprisms exhibited trends comparable to the original Ag nanoprism colloidal dispersion, as demonstrated in Fig. S3(b).
The chemical stability of the synthesised Ag/Pt nanoprisms was assessed by introducing 0.3 mM sodium bromide (NaBr).

Fig. 7 (a) clearly shows that after a 30 min exposure to NaBr, the colloidal dispersion of Ag/Pt nanoprisms retained its original grey appearance and preserved its LSPR properties, as demonstrated in Fig. 7(b). Furthermore, the TEM images in Fig. 7(c) and 7(d) confirm that the Ag/Pt nanoprisms maintained their characteristic triangular shape. This observation proves hybridising Ag nanoprisms with Pt improves their chemical stability, effectively protecting them from Br−-induced chemical etching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of colourimetric sensing probe to detect Pt(II)
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the influence of Pt(II) ion concentration on the etching rate of Ag nanoprisms upon exposure to Br−, Ag nanoprisms were incubated with varying concentrations of Pt(II) ions, ranging from 0.01 to 100 nM, for 30 min at room temperature. The nanoprisms then reacted with 0.3 mM NaBr for 20 min at the same temperature. As illustrated in Fig. 8 (a), the resonance peak of the Ag/Pt hybrid nanoprisms demonstrated a progressive wavelength shift towards shorter wavelengths after exposure to 0.3 mM NaBr. The differential wavelength shift (Δλ) is visually depicted in Fig. 8(b), highlighting the difference in in-plane dipole resonance modes of Ag nanoprisms with and without Pt(II) ions. A clear trend was observed: the wavelength shift (Δλ) increased proportionately to rising Pt(II) ion concentrations, from 0 nM to 100 nM. The inset of Fig. 8(b) displays this effect through a photograph, revealing distinct colour changes in the colloidal dispersion containing Ag/Pt hybrid nanoprisms. Notably, the dispersion exhibited a spectrum of colours, including orange, purple, blue and cyan, depending on the concentration of Pt(II) ions.

This sensing probe exhibits exceptional sensitivity in visually discerning Pt(II) ion content in water, even at ultra-low concentrations as low as 0.1 nM. The LOD was ascertained using the same methodology previously described for Pd(II) ions. The linear equation’s slope, calculated within a concentration span of 0 to 2.5 nM Pt(II) ions, indicated a LOD of 0.3 nM. This result highlights the notably high sensitivity of our Pt(II) ion sensing method, exceeding previously documented results, as summarised in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the sensing probe to a real sample
Setting the context for real sample application
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effectiveness of the developed method, drinking water was chosen as the test sample. The European Agency for the Evaluation of Medicinal Products (EMEA) implies a safety threshold for platinum-group metals, setting a maximum permissible concentration of 5 ppm in total [1]. When converted to Pt(II) ions, this threshold equals 26 µM in drinking water. In our system, with a dilution factor of 12, the safety limit in the total volume decreases to 2.2 µM. Therefore, the sensing probe must be able to detect and distinguish Pt(II) content in drinking water, specifically around the EMEA’s safety limit. For this purpose, experimental conditions were modified to ensure the stability of Pt(II) ions in the concentration range of 0.55–4.4 µM in relation to the NaBr etchant.

As shown in Fig. 8, when the concentration of Pt(II) ions exceeded 100 nM, there was a marked increase in the chemical stability of Ag nanoprisms. This enhanced stability manifested as minimal colour change, even under exposure to 0.3 mM sodium bromide (NaBr). Therefore, to observe a noticeable colour shift within the concentration range of 0.55–4.4 µM Pt(II) ions, it was necessary to modify the experimental conditions. We shortened the incubation time of Ag nanoprisms with Pt(II) ions to 5 min, to limit Pt(II) ion deposition on the surface of Ag nanoprisms. In addition, we increased the NaBr concentration to 0.75 mM and elevated the temperature to 60 °C, aiming to expedite the etching process of Ag nanoprisms. Fig. 9 (a) illustrates the extinction spectra of colloidal dispersions containing Ag nanoprism and varying concentrations of Pt (II) ions. The in-plane dipole resonance mode shows a noticeable blue shift corresponding to decreasing Pt(II) ion concentrations. Inset (a) displays an image of aqueous dispersions containing Ag/Pt nanoprisms. These dispersions were created by adding varying concentrations of Pt(II) ions (0.55 to 4.4 µM) to drinking water, followed by exposure to 0.75 mM NaBr for 8 min at 60 °C. The colloidal dispersion exhibits a range of colours, from cyan and blue to purple and pink, intricately associated with the Pt(II) ion concentration levels. When the concentration of Pt(II) ions aligns with or exceeds the safety limit of 2.2 µM, the dispersions exhibit shades of blue, cyan and green. However, if the Pt(II) ion concentration falls below this limit (specifically at 1.1 and 0.55 µM), the dispersions turn deep purple or purplish-pink. These distinct colour shifts are easily observable with the naked eye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis and comparison
ce:section.ce:para: Quantitative analysis can be conducted using the Δλ value, obtained by subtracting the in-plane dipole resonance mode wavelength of a water sample with Pt(II) ions from the wavelength of the corresponding blank sample, measured at 490 nm. This differential measurement is a reliable metric for evaluating and quantifying the observed changes. To assess the practicality of this method, we compared results from a real water sample with those from a DI water sample. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the Δλ values from a drinking water sample closely match those from DI water. Furthermore, Fig. S4(a) illustrates that colloidal dispersions of Ag nanoprisms with varying Pt(II) ion concentrations (0.55 to 4.4 µM) in DI water exhibit a notable colour shift from an initial grey to vibrant cyan, blue, purple and pink.

This remarkable colour transformation depends on the concentration of Pt(II) ions and occurs when exposed to 0.75 mM sodium bromide (NaBr) at 60 °C for 8 min (Fig. S4(b)). The UV–Vis–NIR extinction spectra of Ag nanoprisms in colloidal dispersions in DI water closely mirror those in the drinking water system, as shown in Fig. S4(c). These findings highlight the notable similarity between the outcomes in analysing actual water samples and deionised (DI) water. The observed correspondence is a positive indication of the potential and versatile utility of the proposed sensing probe, which utilises the structural reorganisation of Ag nanoprisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protocol for a sensing probe to analyse unknown water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Considering these findings, we propose a protocol for a sensing probe to analyse unknown water samples. This protocol entails an initial 12-fold dilution of the sample with pure water, succeeded by adding Ag nanoprisms. Subsequently, the diluted sample undergoes incubation with 0.75 mM NaBr, ensuring a NaBr/Ag molar ratio of approximately 1/2. Following an 8 min incubation period, samples with Pt(II) ion concentrations below the safety threshold will display a distinct colour shift, such as pink or blue. Conversely, samples exceeding the safety threshold for Pt(II) ions will either retain their original dark grey hue or exhibit a subtle shift towards cyan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Use of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in water for efficient and stable bimetallic catalyst
Bimetallic nanocrytsals in catalysis
ce:section.ce:para: Bimetallic nanocrystals often exhibit superior catalytic properties than monometallic ones [23,25]. The synergistic effects of combining Ag with Pd or Pt can result in enhanced catalytic activity [27,45]. As discussed, introducing different metals into a catalyst contributes to its stability, making it more resistant to deactivation or degradation, even under challenging conditions. This increased stability is especially vital in industrial applications, where catalysts face corrosive substances or harsh environments for prolonged periods. Developing efficient water treatment catalysts using Pd(II) or Pt(II) ions in water with Ag nanoprisms promotes both economic and environmental sustainability. This method reduces the environmental impact of mining and extraction by decreasing dependence on newly sourced metals. Ag/Pd or Ag/Pt hybrid nanoprisms, created through the galvanic replacement of Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions with Ag nanoprisms in aqueous solutions, as previously mentioned, can be utilised as catalytic materials for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt nanoprisms
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the catalytic efficiency of Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt hybrid nanoprisms, we conducted the catalytic conversion of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP). This process occurred in an excess sodium borohydride (NaBH4), acting as a model reaction system. 4-NP, widely used in pharmaceuticals, dyes, plastics, pesticides and anticorrosive agents, is acknowledged as a major environmental pollutant owing to its significant toxicity [46]. Consequently, the hydrogenation reaction of 4-NP using NaBH4 to produce 4-AP is a potentially effective and environmentally sustainable approach for removing 4-NP from water sources. This effectiveness is due to 4-AP’s role as a crucial intermediate in various industries and its greater ease of mineralisation and removal from waste streams compared to 4-NP. The initial 4-NP aqueous solution shows an extinction peak at 316 nm, shifting to 400 nm as 4-NP becomes a 4-nitrophenolate ion upon NaBH4 addition (Fig. S5) [47]. After adding catalysts to the model reaction system, the absorption peak at 400 nm gradually decreases. Concurrently, a new absorption peak emerged at 305 nm, growing in intensity over time, indicating successful conversion to 4-AP [48].

The primary catalytic agent used in this study was composed of Ag nanoprisms, as depicted in Fig. 10 (a). UV–visible spectra were methodically captured every 1 min after adding silver nanoprisms to the reaction mixture. The peak extinction intensity at 400 nm steadily decreased, eventually approaching near-zero levels after 12 min of reaction. The transformation of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) was indicated by a new extinction peak at 305 nm. This peak’s intensity progressively increased during the reaction. Notably, complete conversion of 4-NP occurred within just 2 min when Ag/Pd nanoprisms were utilised, as shown in Fig. 10(b)). This indicates a notable improvement in catalytic performance, which can be ascribed to the deposition of Pd onto the Ag nanoprisms. This enhancement is likely due to the synergistic effects of the bimetallic composition. Furthermore, using Ag/Pt hybrid nanoprisms resulted in complete conversion of 4-NP in just 6.5 min, surpassing the performance of Ag nanoprisms alone (refer to Fig. 10(c)). The reaction rate constant (k) was calculated using the pseudo-first-order reaction model [49], assuming an excess NaBH4. This assumption ensures the reaction rate is independent of NaBH4 concentration. In Fig. 10(d), the plot of ln(At /A0 ) versus time t is shown, where At and A0 denote the extinction intensity at time t and initial time, respectively. The calculated reaction rate constants (k) for a 120 s reaction duration are presented as follows: Ag/Pd (21.4 × 10−3 s−1) > Ag/Pt (6.66 × 10−3 s−1) > Ag (0.025 × 10−3 s−1). These results highlight the significant catalytic activity of bimetallic Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt nanoprisms. Although subjected to galvanic substitution reactions under identical concentration and conditions, the higher Pd to Pt ratio in the resulting hybrid nanoprisms (Ag: Pd ≈ 92.2: 7.8 vs. Ag: Pt ≈ 97.8: 2.2) appears to account for the enhanced performance of Ag/Pd nanoprisms over Ag/Pt nanoprisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis of catalytic activity Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt nanoprisms
ce:section.ce:para: To quantitatively compare the catalytic activity of our catalyst with that of other reported catalysts, we calculated the activity parameter κ, defined as the ratio of the rate constant (k) to the total catalyst mass (m). This parameter provides a standardised measure, facilitating effective comparisons across various catalyst systems [45,50,51]. The calculated κ values for Ag, Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt nanoprisms were 1.23 s−1 g−1, 1070 s−1 g−1 and 333 s−1 g−1, respectively. Notably, Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt hybrid nanoprisms exhibited remarkably high catalytic activity, exceeding values reported for heterogeneous catalysts based on metal nanostructures, as illustrated in Table S3. This finding underscores the effectiveness of the hybrid nanoprisms as a catalyst in the reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP, further substantiating their potential utility in catalytic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we developed a novel colourimetric sensing probe tailored to detect Pd(II) and Pt(II) ions in aqueous solutions. This method utilises a custom-designed chemical etching technique for Ag nanoprisms, known for their outstanding plasmonic properties. When exposed to these ions in water, Ag nanoprisms undergo spontaneous surface deposition of Pd or Pt, which is attributed to their higher reduction potentials. This process forms highly stable Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt bimetallic nanoprisms, which maintain their triangular shape even in a strong etchant (NaBr). Intriguingly, the rate of colour and spectral change in these bimetallic nanoprisms, when exposed to NaBr, correlates strongly with the concentration of Pd(II) and Pt(II), enabling highly sensitive and selective colourimetric detection of these ions in water. The proposed sensing probe demonstrates a wide detection range and boasts exceptionally low LODs of 2.6 nM for Pd(II) and 0.3 nM for Pt(II). This probe offers significant advantages, including user-friendliness, cost-effectiveness and compatibility with both naked eye and UV–Vis spectrophotometric detection. Notably, this methodology extends beyond sensitive ion detection and facilitates the on-demand synthesis of tailored bimetallic catalysts. When utilised as heterogeneous catalysts in reducing 4-NP with NaBH4, Ag/Pd and Ag/Pt bimetallic nanoprisms exhibit exceptional catalytic activity. The technology described in this study is believed to possess considerable potential for applications in environmental monitoring, catalysis and resource management.
